item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, and I will translate it into Chinese and post it on SCP CN.When I do all this, I'll put out its links.

This work is short but wonderful,The accidental insertion of advertisement also makes it full of surprises,+1！

Excuse me, my English is not very good,╰(*´︶`*)╯

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is what bugged me too. I remember reading a draft about how a memetic version of the B emoji leaked into the Foundation database, and one of the big issues I had is that the Foundation is essentially a multibillion dollar corporation who (probably) sinks millions into making sure their database in comprehensive. I'd think that the Foundation would avoid screwing with the format unless it was truely necessary (i.e. ../…../../.)

In addition, the containment procedures are a bit OOT. "Water supplies in locations targeted by SCP-4868 are to have [drugs] introduced to them." I could see this having quite a few unintended side effects; what if the entire population of Bumfuck, Texas stopped liking books? I also agree with Pedantique, it's played too straight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Completely fair. I'll admit that I was hesitant about doing a premise like this, since I know there are some very excellent skips that do the trope more justice.

As always, I value your insights and will do better to keep them in mind in future attempts. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's an issue I've had with this contest — in most articles I've seen, people refer EXPLICITLY to the theme, often several times, and it weighs down the work.

And this is a general opinion: it doesn't take much effort to fit an article into each provided theme. Authors shouldn't feel afraid to be more subtle in their writing, even at the expense of people not voting up because it "doesn't fit the theme".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document being affected by the anomaly it's describing just doesn't do anything for me anymore. While a competent execution of the concept, this plays it too straight. I don't feel like there's anything to set it apart from similar articles before it, so I'm at -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I REALLY hate how everyone has to specify the pulp. Just let us assume

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Advertisement-based anomalies affecting their documentation isn't a new trick, but it's done effectively here. You don't immediately clue into the fact that the document is trying to sell you something. One thing I like about this is the dynamic interview presentation, flowing more like a commercial than a proper interview, but selling you on its style because it's written well-enough. +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This was a struggle and I don't know if it turned out well. but I got three passes and here we are.

Valued critters are Weryllium, Ayers, Uncle Nicolini, and Gabriel Jade.

Image is my own, hastily written, super bad fantasy fiction.
